nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs, where it shelters by day in caves, crevices, and overhangs and emerges at night to forage. Has been reported patchily across the central Indo-Pacific, with records from Indonesia, the Philippines, New Caledonia, Hawaii, and tentatively Japan.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock arranged into caves, crevices, and overhangs for daytime shelter
stable rockwork wedged or epoxied to prevent collapse during burrowing
thick gravel or coarse sand bed where the lobster can excavate retreats
secure post-molt hideaway away from tankmates while the new exoskeleton hardens
tightly-fitting lid — reef lobsters are known to climb rockwork and pumps and can escape open-top tanks

diet.notes: Generally considered an easy feeder. A nocturnal scavenger and opportunistic hunter that takes most meaty marine foods: chopped shrimp, mussel, clam, squid, krill, mysis, and sinking pellets or carnivore wafers. Target-feeding with feeding tongs after lights-out is commonly advised to ensure it gets enough food without competing with daytime tankmates. Periodic iodine, calcium, and trace-element supplementation are typically recommended to support successful molts.

goodWith: medium and large robust reef fish that sleep above the substrate (tangs, larger wrasses, angelfish)
large mobile invertebrates that can avoid it
most LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids (when the lobster is well-fed)
established mature reef systems with plenty of rockwork
avoidWith: conspecifics and other reef lobsters — extremely territorial toward each other
small bottom-resting or sleeping fish (gobies, blennies, small wrasses)
ornamental shrimp (cleaner shrimp, peppermint shrimp, sexy shrimp)
small hermit crabs and snails it can crush at night
octopus, mantis shrimp, and other large crustacean predators
any system recently treated with copper-based medications

summary: The Purple Reef Lobster (Enoplometopus debelius) is a striking nocturnal crustacean from the Indo-Pacific, recognized by its pale lavender body, violet polka-dot markings, and orange-tipped claws and legs. Adults reach 10–13 cm. It shelters in caves by day and forages at night — generally considered reef-safe with caution.
careNotes: A reclusive, strictly nocturnal lobster best appreciated as a cryptic reef-tank inhabitant rather than a centerpiece animal — most of the day it stays wedged into a cave or crevice and emerges only after lights-out. Setup should provide abundant live rock arranged into deep caves and overhangs, plus a coarse gravel or sand bed it can excavate; rockwork should be wedged, epoxied, or otherwise stabilised because the lobster is known to undermine loose aquascapes. Generally considered reef safe with caution: it will largely ignore healthy, alert fish but has been reported to grab small fish (gobies, blennies, sleeping wrasses) that rest on the substrate at night, and it will pick off ornamental shrimp, small hermits, and snails — especially if underfed. Strictly one specimen (or, rarely, a confirmed mated pair) per tank; conspecifics fight to the death. Like all marine crustaceans, it is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and any history of copper dosing in the system should be regarded as disqualifying. A tightly-fitting lid is commonly advised: reef lobsters are reported to climb pumps, hoses, and rockwork and have escaped open-top tanks. A tank of at least 200 L (around 50 US gallons) is generally considered a workable minimum, as it allows enough rockwork footprint for caves and a separate post-molt retreat.
breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely reported in home aquaria. Sexes are separate; females carry the fertilised eggs attached to the pleopods until hatching, releasing planktonic larvae that pass through several long-lived pelagic zoeal stages before settlement. Captive rearing is widely regarded as impractical without a dedicated larval-culture setup, and aquarium specimens are essentially all wild-caught from the Indo-Pacific.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied source root resolved via search to the current Debelius' Reef Lobster product page. Confirmed temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, max size ~13 cm, omnivore, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, copper-sensitive, nocturnal.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed Indo-Pacific origin, ~5" max size, omnivore, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe with caution. Stated minimum tank size 50 US gal (~190 L) — used to anchor the cautious 200 L floor used here. Did not provide water-parameter ranges.

additional.name: Aquarium Domain
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), confirmed nocturnal habit, copper sensitivity, molting needs (calcium/iodine/strontium supplementation), and one-specimen-per-tank rule.

additional.name: That Pet Place / That Fish Place
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (5" max), 45 US gal minimum tank, broader temperature tolerance (75–82°F) and salinity 1.022–1.026 used to widen the averaged ranges. Confirmed nocturnal habit and burrowing behaviour.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Reef lobster)
additional.notes: Used only for taxonomy and wild biology: family Enoplometopidae (NOT Nephropidae, as some retailers incorrectly list), order Decapoda; depth range 12–25 m on rocky/coral reefs; patchy Indo-Pacific distribution (Indonesia, New Caledonia, Philippines, Hawaii, possibly Japan); ~12 cm total length. Not used for husbandry parameters.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults are generally reported to molt every one to two months; juveniles molt more frequently. The lobster typically retreats into a cave or burrow to molt and is highly vulnerable until the new exoskeleton hardens — provide a secure post-molt hideaway and resist removing the shed exoskeleton, which can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and iodine levels are commonly cited as supporting successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most-reported cause of failed molts.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females carry fertilised eggs on the pleopods until hatching, releasing long-lived planktonic zoeae that are widely considered impractical to rear in home aquaria. Aquarium specimens are essentially all wild-caught.
